query: Transplantation of Human Organs Act eligibility criteria for declaring brainstem death

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Death is defined in the United States by the Uniform Determination of Death Act (UDDA), proposed in 1981. A determination of death must be made in accordance with the accepted medical standards and must additionally include one of the following: Irreversible cessation of circulatory and pulmonary functions. Irreversible cessation of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem, is dead.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is essential to distinguish the term "brain death" from "coma" to the public, as coma may imply a limited form of life. The understanding that brain death is equivalent to death helps guide decision-making for both physicians and patients' families regarding the withdrawal of care and prevents the unnecessary expenditure of resources. An essential topic that evolved in parallel with brain death is obtaining organs for transplantation. According to the "dead donor rule," organ procurement can occur only after death has been declared. Therefore, for patients who are brain dead, the procurement of viable organs is allowed, even if a patient still has some circulatory and/or pulmonary function. This concept continues to result in some ongoing debate and controversy. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A previous international study of brain death criteria in 91 countries by Patel PV et al. showed that 22% of low-income vs. 97% of high-income countries had an institutional protocol for brain death determination. [11] The same study also showed variability in apnea test performance and concluded that countries with an organized transplant network were more likely to have a brain death provision when compared to countries without such provisions.

retrieved_docs.title: Death Certification -- Other Issues -- Certification of Brain Death
retrieved_docs.content: A single physician with institutional privileges can certify brain death. If the question of organ donation arises, the state of New York requires that the brain death certification be done by the physician who attended to the patient at the time of death and another physician. Both physicians should confirm that the clinical evaluation meets an accepted medical standard. All the processes and phases involved in the brain death determination should be properly documented: The cause of irreversibility of coma and unresponsiveness The absence of motor response to noxious stimuli The absence of brainstem reflexes in 2 separate examinations separated by at least 6 hours. The absence of respiration with pCO greater than 60 mm Hg The rationale and justification for ancillary studies and confirmatory test

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue and Organ Donation -- Issues of Concern -- Tissue and Organ Selection and Processing
retrieved_docs.content: Members of the healthcare team should have a clear understanding of the issues involved in tissue and organ donation as this will improve outcomes. Organ donation starts with confirmation of clinical brain death, usually by a neurosurgeon or neurologist.  It should be remembered that a coma is not brain death. In general, this is based on accepted medical standards: Irreversible cessation of brain functions, including the brain stem [12] [13] [14] Irreversible cessation of circulatory and respiratory functions Other considerations for death may include family or patient has signed an advanced directive to withdraw life support.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- III. Ancillary Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral angiography: Four-vessel angiography is the gold standard for tests evaluating cerebral blood flow. It can confirm brain death when it shows cessation of blood flow to the brain. Limitations include the invasiveness of the test and transferring the patient to the radiology suite. Also, the contrast may induce nephrotoxicity affecting the donor's kidney. False-negative tests can occur when ICP becomes lowered by surgery, trauma, or ventricular shunts.

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Death (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Public belief in brain death and organ procurement - There can be a significant concern among the relatives and the public that organ donation occurs when the patient heart is still beating, and the person is not entirely dead. There can be looming fear that death will be declared prematurely for the sake of organ and tissue retrieval. [7] Is the brain dead person really dead? - Issues in defining biological death - certain pitfalls merit consideration while evaluating for brainstem death confirmation: [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The UDDA was drafted in the late 1970s in response to medical advances in life support that allowed for complete circulatory and respiratory support despite complete cessation of brain function. The UDDA did not establish what "accepted medical standards" were; instead, only that they existed. The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) published the initial standards in 1995, which were updated in 2010 to the current standards.

retrieved_docs.title: Organ Procurement From Patients With Trauma -- Clinical Significance -- Donation After Circulatory Death
retrieved_docs.content: Circulatory death is defined as the permanent absence of respiration and circulation and is increasingly utilized to expand the pool of potential organ donors. Typically, donation after circulatory death patients have suffered a neurologic injury but do not meet brain death criteria. Less commonly, patients with respiratory or neurodegenerative diseases may electively withdraw treatment. Initially restricted to kidney transplants, this practice has expanded to include liver, lung, and pancreatic transplants. One of the primary challenges of donation after circulatory death is balancing the assurance of donor death while minimizing ischemic damage to the intended organs.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Time of death is also country and institutional guideline-based. As per AAN, the time of death is when PCO2 reaches the target value during the apnea test. For patients who underwent the ancillary examination, death is when the results are officially reported. [14] However, in the United Kingdom, although two exams are needed to diagnose the test formally, the legal time of death is when the first test confirms the absence of brainstem reflexes. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Kidney Transplantation -- Preparation -- Donor Selection/Allocation
retrieved_docs.content: Deceased donors are broken down into those that are brain dead (DBD) and those that donate after cardiac death (DCD).  As the term would indicate, brain dead donors are those that have satisfied formal criteria for brain death testing.  DCD donors are patients who, while they do not meet the criteria for formal brain death, are deemed by neurologists as being unlikely to experience a meaningful neurologic recovery. [18] In the case of DCD donation, procurement cannot begin until the heart has stopped beating, and an independent physician pronounces the patient following terminal extubation.  Efforts by the United Network for Organ Sharing (UNOS) to further stratify deceased donors were delineated to reflect likely organ quality.  Thus, deceased donors were defined as meeting standard criteria (SCD) or otherwise fell under the umbrella of extended criteria donation (ECD). ECD kidneys are associated with shortened graft longevity secondary to donor risk factors: age over 60, or those between 50 to 59 years of age with a history of hypertension, terminal creatinine concentration above 1.5 mg/dL, or cerebrovascular cause of death. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The largest known study to date regarding brain death determination protocols worldwide was released in 2020 by Lewis A. et al. 136 corresponding countries' contacts (42% world) revealed high variability in the brain death criteria protocols internationally. [10] Eighty-three countries had brain death protocols, while 53 did not. Of the 83 countries with protocols, 78 had unique protocols. This study also determined a considerable difference between clinical exam components of the brain death examination. The pupillary light reflex was included in 70 (90%) of the 83 countries, 68 (87%) included the corneal reflex, 67 (86%) included the oculovestibular reflex, 64 (82%) included the gag reflex, 62 (79%) included the cough reflex, 58 (74%) included the oculocephalic reflex, 37 (47%) included noxious stimulation to the face, 22 (28%) included noxious stimulation to the limbs, and 22 (28%) included other reflexes. While the apnea test was a requirement in 91% of protocols, it is of interest that there were differences in the apnea testing technique and reference ranges for determining brain death, including target partial pressure of carbon dioxide in arterial blood (PaCO2). This ancillary test was deemed a requirement to pronounce brain death in 22% of protocols. [10]

retrieved_docs.content: Death is defined in the United States by the Uniform Determination of Death Act (UDDA), proposed in 1981. A determination of death must be made in accordance with the accepted medical standards and must additionally include one of the following: Irreversible cessation of circulatory and pulmonary functions. Irreversible cessation of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem, is dead.

retrieved_docs.content: Distinguishing the term "brain death" from "coma" to the public is essential, as coma may imply a limited form of life. The understanding that brain death is equivalent to death helps guide decision-making for both physicians and patients' families regarding the withdrawal of care and prevents the unnecessary expenditure of resources. An essential topic that evolved in parallel with brain death is obtaining organs for transplantation. retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Death (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Brainstem death is a clinical diagnosis made by an examiner. Ancillary tests are not essential for confirming brain death. [1] Brainstem death has to be certified by certified board members, which include: Medical superintendent (MS) - in-charge of the hospital The treating medical or critical care specialist A neurologist or a neurosurgeon Members from the organ donation or the transplantation team cannot be involved in this certification. The clinical diagnosis of brain death should take place in three steps: Establishing the etiology Excluding possible reversible syndromes that may produce signs similar to brain death Demonstration of clinical signs of brain death including coma, brainstem areflexia, and apnea However, when planning for organ donation, separate complete examinations by two physicians is recommended. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Death Criteria (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: AAN's recent position statement on brain death endorses UDDA's death definition as "irreversible loss of entire brain, including the brainstem, has been determined by the demonstration of complete loss of consciousness (coma), brainstem reflexes, and the independent capacity for the ventilatory drive (apnea), in the absence of any factors that imply possible reversibility." [1] Irreversibility in the definition of death refers to the impossibility of recovery, regardless of any medical intervention, which requires clear elaboration, as with the advancement of mechanical ventilation and life support technologies during the 20th century, patients who suffered severe brain damage could be maintained physiologically for prolonged periods in intensive care units (ICUs). [2] [3] AAN, in addition, believes "preserved neuroendocrine function may be present despite irreversible injury of the cerebral hemispheres and brainstem and is not inconsistent with the whole brain standard of death."

Major therapeutic categories, including المجازة القلبية الرئوية (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) and المعالجة بالأدوية (drug therapy), accept all four qualifiers, underscoring the integration of ethical considerations, methodological standards, quantitative evidence, and trend monitoring across patient care. retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is used as a permissible descriptor within a classification framework for a diverse set of medical entities. It is applied to the classification of arboviry (arboviruses), the conversion of cardiac rhythm, and diagnostic amniocentesis, indicating that these infectious agents, cardiac procedural outcomes, and prenatal diagnostic techniques can be qualified in a standardized way. The same “Allowed qualifier” also extends to the classification of الارتجاج المخي (cerebral concussion) and كسر في النخاع الشوكي (spinal cord fracture), showing its relevance for traumatic neurological injuries. Additionally, it is used in the classification of organ transplantation (other than bone marrow), reflecting its role in categorizing transplant procedures that fall outside the bone‑marrow category. Finally, the “Allowed qualifier” applies to the classification of إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى (abortion accompanied by infection), demonstrating its utility in obstetric complications. Together, these applications illustrate that the “Allowed qualifier” serves as a flexible, standardized element for coding, documenting, and managing a wide range of conditions and procedures across infectious disease, cardiology, prenatal care, trauma, transplantation, and obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2722
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier includes a range of medical interventions—antagonists of antidiuretic hormone, temporary and permanent colostomy, diagnostic amniocentesis, myelogram, organ transplantation (other than bone marrow), other respiratory therapy, and peritoneal dialysis—and each of these interventions is associated with harmful side effects. Furthermore, those harmful side effects are linked to illegal induced abortion. Recognizing the potential adverse effects of these procedures and therapies is essential for informed clinical decision‑making and patient safety, particularly when illegal abortion may be a related concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is used as a classification attribute for a diverse set of medical procedures and interventions. It applies to the classification (klasifikace) of temporary and permanent colostomy, nephrotomy, spinal fusion, myelogram, organ transplantation (other than bone marrow), peritoneal dialysis, radionuclides, illegal induced abortion (إجهاض محرض غير قانوني), medical loop application (تطبيق علقة طبية), and tooth extraction (قلع الأسنان). This indicates that each of these procedures is recognized within a specific classification framework where the allowed qualifier defines their permissible status or categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2138
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل (جراحي)) is classified as a member of the broader clinical grouping “termination of pregnancy and risk of miscarriage” (إنهاء الحمل وخطر الإجهاض (SMQ)). This relationship indicates that the specific procedure of surgically ending a pregnancy is encompassed within the larger safety‑monitoring and terminology framework that addresses both pregnancy termination methods and the associated risk of miscarriage, facilitating comprehensive categorization and assessment in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1472
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a concept that can be applied to a variety of medical and clinical contexts. It serves as a permissible descriptor for procedures such as conversion of cardiac rhythm and diagnostic amniocentesis, indicating that these interventions may be performed under specific qualifying conditions. In addition, the term dějiny, which denotes “history,” is linked to a broad spectrum of events and procedures relevant to patient care and public health. Dějiny is associated with heterosexual sexual contact (اتصال جنسي مغاير), both temporary and permanent gastrostomy, myelogram examinations, organ transplantation procedures that exclude bone marrow, and tympanoplasty for middle‑ear reconstruction. It also relates to sensitive and legally significant situations such as illegal induced abortion (إجهاض محرض غير قانوني) and traffic accidents (حادث مروري). Together, these connections illustrate how allowed qualifiers and the historical context of dějiny intersect with diverse medical interventions, diagnostic techniques, surgical procedures, and socio‑legal circumstances, underscoring the importance of clear qualification criteria in clinical decision‑making and documentation.
